topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The bouquet landed on the worn wooden table with a soft thud. They were bright, almost offensively cheerful—a riot of crimson roses and yellow sunflowers. I didn't move my hand to pick them up immediately. My gaze drifted from the vibrant petals to the chipped rim of my coffee mug, tracing the hairline fracture in the glaze. A faint sigh escaped my lips, barely audible above the hum of the distant traffic. I let my eyes linger on the arrangement for a long moment, cataloging the colors without assigning any real meaning to them. I finally reached out, my fingers brushing the velvety texture of a deep red bloom, then withdrew them as if the contact had been slightly irritating. There was no note, just the flowers, placed there on my otherwise unremarkable Tuesday afternoon.

[story 2]

When the delivery person handed over the wrapping paper, I simply nodded, a small, practiced movement. My expression remained neutral, my eyes fixed on the street outside the window, watching a particularly slow jogger pass by. The arrangement was undeniably pretty, expensive even, yet it registered with me as just another piece of unsolicited decoration. I took the box, the paper crinkling under my grip, and carried it toward the kitchen counter. I set it down next to the stack of unopened mail, not bothering to examine the stems or the arrangement itself. I poured myself a glass of water, the cool liquid a familiar comfort against the slight tightness in my chest. It was just an object, temporarily occupying space.

[story 3]

I found the flowers sitting on my porch railing when I came out to retrieve the mail. They were elaborate, almost theatrical, a cascade of purple and white blooms that seemed far too dramatic for the quiet suburban street. I stopped halfway to the door, my shoulders slumping almost imperceptibly. I looked at the arrangement, then up at the sky, which was a flat, unremarkable grey. I didn't feel a surge of warmth, nor any sudden spark of interest. Instead, I simply took a step back, observing the spectacle from a distance, a detached assessment of its existence. I let the silence stretch, waiting for the moment to pass so I could go inside and deal with the actual necessities of the day.
